title: Can machine learning predict the liquidus temperature of binary alloys?
ml_models: linear regression, random forest, deep neural network
ml_models_quote:
linear regression — "We employ three fundamental machine learning (ML) models: linear regressions [68], random forest [69], and deep neural networks models [70]."
random forest — "A Random Forest (RF) [4] model is an ensemble machine learning method that builds multiple decision trees (tree whose branches determine certain “decisions”, typically splitting by feature values) and combines their predictions to improve accuracy and reduce overfitting (a phenomenon when a model learns to fit training data too closely and fails to generalize to unseen data)."
deep neural network — "Further, our data reveal that there is not a considerable difference between using a simple nonlinear model like a Random Forest and a more complicated model like a well-tuned neural network model."
target_properties_quote: liquidus temperature — "majority voting for classification or averaging for regression, which is our context, since we learn to predict liquidus temperatures of alloys."
dataset_size_quote: The considered data originate from 429 binary alloy systems and each alloy system is represented by ∼ 201 compositions resulting in a total of 85,523 binary alloys altogether.
data_type_quote: To estimate  (experimental determined liquidus temperature), this benchmark model carries out a linear interpolation in Fig.
features: Physically Informed (PI) features, Elemental Properties (EP) features, Composition Fractions (Comp) features, Random (Rand) features, Difference in melting temperature, Heat of mixing, Crystal structure mismatch
features_quote:
Physically Informed (PI) features — "One is the Physically Informed feature vector that considers the mixing of the elements based on the corresponding elemental properties and specific mixing characteristics."
Elemental Properties (EP) features — "The other is the Elemental Properties feature vector that only considers the properties of the constituent elements of the alloy."
Composition Fractions (Comp) features — "For benchmarking and comparison, we create Composition Fractions (Comp) features which only consider the composition of an alloy but don’t include information about the alloys’ properties."
Random (Rand) features — "As an additional benchmark feature, we also consider Random (Rand) features, i.e."
Difference in melting temperature — "(4) Difference in melting temperature: (5) Δ T = | T A − T B | p A · T A + p B · T B (5) Heat of mixing [65]: (6) H = ϵ A B · p A · p B where ϵ A B is calculated using Miedema’s model for the pairs of elements in the alloys [66]."
Heat of mixing — "(4) Difference in melting temperature: (5) Δ T = | T A − T B | p A · T A + p B · T B (5) Heat of mixing [65]: (6) H = ϵ A B · p A · p B where ϵ A B is calculated using Miedema’s model for the pairs of elements in the alloys [66]."
Crystal structure mismatch — "(7) Crystal structure mismatch: (8) C S M = C S · p A · p B CS = 0 if the crystal structures of A and B are the same and CS = 1 if the crystal structures of A and B are different."
num_features_explanation: The Physically Informed (PI) feature set, which is the primary best-performing set, consists of seven descriptors as explicitly stated: 'PI feature set consists of a compact set of seven descriptors that explicitly include alloy mixing information' and 'We describe below the seven Physically Informed features which constitute the Physically Informed feature vector describing the mixing of the constituent elements in binary alloys and its effect on the thermal stability.'
performance_metric_quote: When we use these 2 feature sets with their original form to predict the liquidus temperature of binary alloys, although PI feature set has significantly lower dimensionality than EP feature set, PI features (MAPE = 7.96%) consistently outperform EP features (MAPE = 8.85%).
confidence_explanation: All core fields were found and supported by verbatim quotes. The ML models (linear regression, random forest, deep neural network), target property (liquidus temperature), dataset size (85,523 alloys), features (PI, EP, Comp, Rand, and individual descriptors), num_features (7 for PI), and performance metric (MAPE) are all clearly stated. Data type is experimental since liquidus temperatures are experimentally determined and DFT/CALPHAD are used for additional features/comparison.
